cards.title: The Hospitality Industry and its Sectors
lines: Hospitality means providing food, drink, and accommodation services to customers.
The industry operates across three sectors: commercial, voluntary, and public.
Commercial businesses run for profit, such as hotels, restaurants, and pubs.
Public sector providers include schools, colleges, and hospitals.
Voluntary sector providers are charities and non-profit organisations.
The industry contributes to the GDP of the UK through jobs, spending, and tourism.

cards.title: Types of Business Ownership
lines: A sole trader is a business owned and run by one person who keeps all the profit.
A partnership is owned by two or more people who share profit and responsibility.
A franchise lets others buy into an existing business and use its business model.
A limited liability partnership protects owners so they only lose what they invested.
Businesses in hospitality inter-relate, for example a hotel buying from local suppliers.

cards.title: Food Safety, Pathogens & Safe Temperatures
lines: Food safety and hygiene means keeping food free from contamination that can cause illness.
Pathogenic bacteria that affect food include Salmonella, E-Coli, and Staph Aureus.
A deep-freezer must operate at minus 18 degrees Celsius.
A refrigerator must operate between 0 and 5 degrees Celsius.
Hot food must be held above 63 degrees Celsius, but 75 degrees Celsius is preferable.
Hot food should be chilled quickly before being stored safely in a refrigerator.

cards.title: Cooking Methods and Commodities
lines: Roasting is a dry method that adds oil and cooks food at 170 to 185 degrees Celsius.
Baking cooks food in a hot oven, while grilling uses direct radiant heat.
Braising cooks meat slowly in a small amount of liquid such as stock or water.
Steaming cooks food over boiling water, for example vegetables in a bamboo steamer.
Poaching cooks food gently in liquid at around 75 degrees Celsius.
Poultry is trussed, meaning tied into shape, before it is cooked.

cards.title: Internal, External Customers & Their Needs
lines: An internal customer is a colleague from another department, such as a commis chef.
An external customer is someone outside the business, such as a paying guest.
Provide effective service by being polite and respectful when greeting customers.
Be attentive to customer needs during service and offer assistance when required.
Customer feedback can be gathered through surveys, comment cards, and reviews.

cards.title: Allergen Awareness and Accessibility
lines: There are fourteen named allergens on the regulatory list from the Food Standards Agency.
Common allergens include gluten, shellfish, and mustard.
Awareness can be raised with an allergen factsheet shared online and through staff training.
Customers with mobility issues can be supported with ramps and dedicated entry points.
Customers with hearing difficulties can be supported by installing hearing loops.

cards.title: Food Groups, Nutrition and Exercise
lines: The main food commodities are meat, vegetables, poultry, fish, and shellfish.
A balanced diet incorporates different food groups to supply nutrients, minerals, and vitamins.
Foods can be preserved by being dried, frozen, vacuum-packed, cured, or canned.
Preserving food can reduce some of its nutritional value.
Regular exercise benefits both the human body and the human mind.

cards.title: Teamwork, Dynamics and Conflict
lines: Working well in a team maximises the success of the team and the business overall.
Positive relationships are encouraged through trust building and getting to know you sessions.
Negative working relationships can lower team morale and harm the business image.
Conflict can emerge from disagreements between staff members during service.
Conflict can be managed through mediation, mentoring, and regular team meetings.

cards.title: Papers, Objectives and Grading
lines: The qualification is assessed by Paper 1, Paper 2-A, and Paper 2-B.
Paper 1 has multiple choice and short answer questions and lasts one hour.
Paper 2-A has scenario based questions and lasts one hour.
Paper 2-B is a portfolio assessment that includes a practical assessment task.
The guided learning hours are 130, and results are reported on a grade scale of 9 to 1.
